pages.full_text: it became a prison (there was an oubliette) & is now a museum & a place for meetings. There was an exhibition of giant photos of insects. Stag beetles etc. The town still has the richness of centuries ago in some of its buildings & main streets. Pleasantly has- been. We returned to the camp & bought good chips to go with supp[er]
photo_captions: Joseph Albers. Study to homage the Square 1956. In the museum of Antonio + Carmela Calderara. Ameno.
pages.notes: Page contains a glued-in art print of Josef Albers 'Study to homage the Square 1956'. The caption is written vertically along the right side of the print. The narrative text at the bottom continues from a previous page (mid-sentence). No handwritten page number visible in the top-left corner. The last word of the text is cut off — reads 'go with supp[er]' or similar; transcribed as far as legible. 'Pleasantly has- been' appears to be an informal expression meaning the town retains a pleasantly old-fashioned character. Place name 'Ameno' appears only in the photo caption.

pages.full_text: Saturday I got another load of wash done which has just about dried before 4pm rain. Humid. We drive along old roads to Vacciago (which was open this time) & saw the Calderara museum, a bit like Kettles Yard. Rather sad, as their only daughter had died, His
pages.notes: Handwritten page number '21' visible in top-right corner, consistent with B_0199_R showing page 19. Page contains a photograph of a courtyard/house, captioned 'Lovely old house of Calderara. 1903-76.' — the dates likely refer to the life of Antonio Calderara (1903–1978 or similar; transcribed as written '1903-76'). Text ends mid-sentence with 'His' — continues to following spread. 'Kettles Yard' refers to the art house/gallery in Cambridge, England, used as a comparison for the Calderara museum in Vacciago. Year cannot be determined from this page alone; context from B_0199 confirms this is an Italy trip near Orta San Giulio/Vacciago.
